clues: This poet's early works include the masque "Comus" and "Lycidas," a pastoral elegy mourning his drowned classmate Edward King.
In the 1644 prose tract "Areopagitica," he argued passionately against the licensing and censorship of books.
Now blind, he reflected on his condition in the sonnet beginning "When I consider how my light is spent."
He dictated his greatest work to his daughters after losing his sight entirely.
Name this 17th-century English poet who wrote the epic "Paradise Lost" about the fall of Adam and Eve.
This organelle carries its own small circular genome and is believed to have arisen when an ancient proteobacterium was engulfed in an endosymbiotic event.
Its highly folded inner membrane forms shelf-like structures called cristae, dramatically increasing surface area.
It is the principal site of the citric acid cycle and of oxidative phosphorylation along the electron transport chain.
Through cellular respiration, it produces the bulk of a eukaryotic cell's ATP.
Frequently dubbed "the powerhouse of the cell," name this organelle.
As a young artillery officer, this man first won fame for driving British forces out during the 1793 Siege of Toulon.
To finance his European ambitions, he sold the vast Louisiana Territory to the United States in 1803.
His massive Grande Armée was shattered by winter and attrition during an ill-fated 1812 invasion of Russia.
Defeated at the 1815 Battle of Waterloo, he was exiled to the remote island of Saint Helena, where he died.
Name this general who crowned himself Emperor of the French in 1804.
Its Blue tributary rises at Lake Tana in the Ethiopian Highlands and merges with its White tributary at Khartoum.
The Aswan High Dam, completed in 1970, tamed its flow and created the reservoir known as Lake Nasser.
For millennia, civilizations along its banks relied on its predictable annual flooding to deposit fertile black silt.
It runs northward through Egypt before fanning out into a broad delta on the Mediterranean Sea.
Name this African waterway long regarded as the longest river in the world.
While voluntarily committed to an asylum at Saint-Rémy-de-Provence, this painter produced canvases of irises and cypress trees.
He briefly shared the "Yellow House" in Arles with Paul Gauguin, a stay that ended in a violent quarrel.
According to the famous story, he severed part of his own ear during a mental breakdown.
His view of a village beneath a turbulent, swirling night sky is titled "The Starry Night."
Name this Dutch Post-Impressionist who painted a celebrated series of "Sunflowers."
This composer labored for years over his single opera, "Fidelio," producing several different overtures for it.
He originally dedicated his Third Symphony, the "Eroica," to Napoleon, then furiously scratched out the dedication.
The choral finale of his Ninth Symphony sets Friedrich Schiller's "Ode to Joy."
He continued to compose masterpieces even as he went almost completely deaf.
Name this German composer of the "Moonlight Sonata" and the bagatelle "Für Elise."
This novel opens with an "Etymology" section and a set of "Extracts" supposedly gathered by a "sub-sub-librarian."
Its narrator alone survives the wreck by clinging to a coffin built for the harpooner Queequeg.
Captain Ahab loses his leg to, and obsessively hunts, a white whale.
It famously begins, "Call me Ishmael."
Name this Herman Melville novel about the hunt for a great white whale.
reject: The Old Man and the Sea

clues: This novel was originally drafted under the title "First Impressions."
The clergyman Mr. Collins proposes to the heroine and, refused, soon marries her friend Charlotte Lucas.
It opens, "It is a truth universally acknowledged, that a single man in possession of a good fortune, must be in want of a wife."
Its heroine Elizabeth Bennet overcomes her dislike of the proud Mr. Darcy.
Name this Jane Austen novel about the Bennet sisters.
In this novel a green light at the end of a dock symbolizes the protagonist’s yearning for the past.
Its narrator Nick Carraway rents a small house in West Egg next to a mysterious millionaire.
The story climaxes with a fatal car crash that kills Myrtle Wilson.
Jay Gatsby throws extravagant parties hoping to win back Daisy Buchanan.
Name this F. Scott Fitzgerald novel set in the Jazz Age.
This author’s unfinished novels "Amerika" and "The Castle" were published posthumously by his friend Max Brod, against his stated wishes.
In one of his novels, the clerk Josef K. is arrested and prosecuted by an inscrutable authority.
In his best-known story, the salesman Gregor Samsa wakes up transformed into a giant insect.
An adjective from his name describes nightmarishly opaque bureaucracy.
Name this German-language author from Prague who wrote "The Metamorphosis."
This writer’s only complete novel is "The Narrative of Arthur Gordon Pym of Nantucket."
He is often credited with inventing detective fiction through his sleuth C. Auguste Dupin.
His tales of terror include "The Tell-Tale Heart" and "The Fall of the House of Usher."
In one poem, a bird perched on a bust of Pallas repeatedly croaks "Nevermore."
Name this American author of "The Raven."
Chargaff’s rules state that within this molecule the amount of adenine equals that of thymine, and guanine equals cytosine.
Rosalind Franklin’s X-ray diffraction image "Photo 51" was key to revealing its structure.
James Watson and Francis Crick described its double-helix structure in 1953.
Its four bases are adenine, thymine, guanine, and cytosine.
Name this molecule, abbreviated with three letters, that stores genetic information.
This scientist advanced a corpuscular theory of light and built the first practical reflecting telescope.
His masterwork, the "Principia Mathematica," set out three laws of motion.
He and Gottfried Leibniz independently developed calculus.
A falling apple supposedly inspired his law of universal gravitation.
Name this English physicist with three eponymous laws of motion.
The light-independent reactions of this process fix carbon dioxide through the Calvin cycle.
Its light-dependent reactions split water and release oxygen within the thylakoid membranes.
It takes place in chloroplasts and relies on the green pigment chlorophyll.
Through it, plants turn sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide into glucose.
Name this process by which green plants make their own food from light.
Allotropes of this element include buckminsterfullerene and the single-layer sheet graphene.
Radiocarbon dating relies on a radioactive isotope of this element with mass number 14.
Having atomic number 6, it forms the backbone of organic chemistry.
Both diamond and graphite are pure forms of it.
Name this element with the chemical symbol C.
This physicist’s explanation of the photoelectric effect won him the 1921 Nobel Prize in Physics.
During his 1905 "miracle year" he also published a paper explaining Brownian motion.
His general theory of relativity describes gravity as the curvature of spacetime.
He is famous for the mass-energy equivalence equation E = mc².
Name this German-born physicist known for the theory of relativity.
The outer boundary of one of these objects is its event horizon, surrounding a central singularity.
Stephen Hawking predicted that they slowly emit a faint thermal radiation now named for him.
One can form when a sufficiently massive star collapses at the end of its life.
In 2019 the Event Horizon Telescope released the first image of one, at the heart of galaxy M87.
Name this region of spacetime whose gravity is so strong that not even light can escape.
This man’s account of his own military campaign opens by noting that all Gaul is divided into three parts.
In 49 BC he crossed the Rubicon, reportedly declaring "the die is cast."
He was assassinated on the Ides of March, 44 BC, by a group of senators including Brutus.
The titles "Kaiser" and "Tsar," and the month of July, all derive from his name.
Name this Roman general and dictator.
Legend says this ruler had herself smuggled in to meet Julius Caesar concealed within bedding or a carpet.
She was the last active monarch of the Greek-speaking Ptolemaic dynasty.
She formed a political and romantic alliance with the Roman general Mark Antony.
She is said to have died from the bite of an asp.
Name this famous final queen of ancient Egypt.
Built starting in 1961, this barrier’s longer stretch ran through countryside as the "Hinterland" section.
Checkpoint Charlie was its best-known crossing point.
In 1987 Ronald Reagan stood before it and demanded, "Mr. Gorbachev, tear down this wall!"
It was opened on November 9, 1989, paving the way for German reunification.
Name this wall that long divided a single German city in two.
In 1858 this man sharpened his reputation in a series of debates against Stephen Douglas.
He issued the Emancipation Proclamation in 1863.
At a battlefield cemetery dedication he delivered an address beginning "Four score and seven years ago."
He was assassinated by John Wilkes Booth at Ford’s Theatre in 1865.
Name this 16th U.S. president who led the Union through the Civil War.
Born Temüjin, this leader endured a harsh youth, including a period in captivity, before uniting the steppe tribes.
His body of laws was known as the Yassa.
The empire he founded grew into the largest contiguous land empire in history.
He was proclaimed the first Great Khan of the Mongols around 1206.
Name this Mongol conqueror, the grandfather of Kublai Khan.
This peak lies in the Mahalangur Himal sub-range and is called Chomolungma in Tibetan.
It was first conclusively summited in 1953 by Edmund Hillary and Tenzing Norgay.
It straddles the border between Nepal and the Tibet region of China.
Climbers must endure a "Death Zone" above 8,000 meters near its summit.
Name this highest mountain on Earth.
The Tibesti and Ahaggar mountain ranges rise out of this region.
The nomadic Tuareg people have long crossed it by camel caravan.
Stretching across much of North Africa, it is the largest hot desert in the world.
Its name comes directly from the Arabic word for "desert."
Name this vast desert covering most of northern Africa.
Near Manaus, this river’s dark Rio Negro tributary meets a paler one at the famous "Meeting of Waters."
It rises in the Andes of Peru and empties into the Atlantic Ocean.
By volume of water discharged, it is the largest river on Earth.
It winds through the world’s largest rainforest, mostly within Brazil.
Name this South American river that rivals the Nile as the world’s longest.
This country’s arid interior, the "Outback," contains the great sandstone monolith Uluru.
The Great Barrier Reef lies off its northeastern coast.
Its capital is Canberra, though its largest city is Sydney.
It is home to marsupials such as kangaroos and koalas.
Name this country that is also the smallest continent.
Straddling the Mid-Atlantic Ridge, this country taps abundant geothermal energy.
The 2010 eruption of its volcano Eyjafjallajökull grounded much of European air travel.
Its legislature, the Althing, is one of the oldest parliaments in the world.
Its capital and largest city is Reykjavík.
Name this Nordic island nation nicknamed the "Land of Fire and Ice."
This polymath filled notebooks written in mirror script with sketches of flying machines and anatomy.
His mural "The Last Supper" covers a wall of a convent in Milan.
His drawing "Vitruvian Man" inscribes a figure within a circle and a square.
He painted a portrait celebrated for its enigmatic smile, the "Mona Lisa."
Name this Italian Renaissance master.
An early sculpture by this artist, a "Pietà," sits in St. Peter’s Basilica.
Decades later he painted the swirling "Last Judgment" on a chapel’s altar wall.
He spent roughly four years painting that chapel’s ceiling for Pope Julius II.
His towering marble "David" stands in Florence.
Name this Italian Renaissance sculptor and painter of the Sistine Chapel.
Around 1907, alongside Georges Braque, this artist developed a radically fragmented style.
His mural protesting the bombing of a Basque town is titled "Guernica."
His early career is often split into a "Blue Period" and a "Rose Period."
He is regarded as a co-founder of Cubism.
Name this Spanish painter of "Les Demoiselles d’Avignon."
This painter made entire series depicting haystacks and the facade of Rouen Cathedral under changing light.
His final decades centered on the water-lily ponds of his garden at Giverny.
An entire art movement took its name from his canvas "Impression, Sunrise."
He was a leading French Impressionist.
Name this painter of the "Water Lilies," whose surname is often confused with that of Édouard Manet.
This Norwegian artist explored love, anxiety, and death in a cycle of works called the "Frieze of Life."
He described being seized by an "infinite scream passing through nature" during a walk at sunset.
His signature image shows a hairless figure clutching its face on a bridge beneath a lurid orange sky.
That painting is titled "The Scream."
Name this pioneering Expressionist painter.
This composer’s final work, a Requiem in D minor, was left unfinished at his death and completed by a pupil.
His operas include "The Magic Flute" and "Don Giovanni."
A child prodigy, he toured the courts of Europe performing from a very young age.
His life and rivalry with Salieri were dramatized in the film "Amadeus."
Name this Austrian composer of "Eine kleine Nachtmusik."
This composer wrote a set of preludes and fugues in every key, "The Well-Tempered Clavier."
His six "Brandenburg Concertos" were dedicated to a German margrave.
A devout Lutheran, he composed the monumental "Mass in B minor."
His surname is the German word for "brook."
Name this Baroque composer of the "Toccata and Fugue in D minor."
This group pushed studio experimentation on the 1966 album "Revolver," including "Tomorrow Never Knows."
Their 1967 concept album was "Sgt. Pepper’s Lonely Hearts Club Band."
Managed by Brian Epstein, they inspired a frenzy of fandom dubbed with their own name plus "-mania."
Its four members were John, Paul, George, and Ringo.
Name this rock band from Liverpool.
This composer collaborated with Diaghilev’s Ballets Russes on "The Firebird" and "Petrushka."
The 1913 Paris premiere of one of his ballets reportedly provoked a near-riot in the audience.
That ballet, depicting a pagan sacrificial rite, is "The Rite of Spring."
He was a hugely influential 20th-century Russian-born composer.
Name this composer of "The Rite of Spring."
This composer’s "1812 Overture" famously calls for the firing of cannon.
His ballets include "Swan Lake" and "The Sleeping Beauty."
His Christmas ballet contains the "Dance of the Sugar Plum Fairy."
That ballet is "The Nutcracker."
Name this Russian Romantic composer.
In this novel the Ministry of Truth employs Winston Smith to rewrite history, and a language called Newspeak shrinks each year to limit thought.
Citizens practice "doublethink," and dissenters are broken in the dreaded Room 101.
Its citizens are watched by telescreens under the slogan "Big Brother is watching you."
It is set in a totalitarian superstate called Oceania.
Name this dystopian novel by George Orwell, titled with a year.
A friar’s sleeping potion and a mistimed message drive this play’s tragic ending.
It is set in Verona and concerns the feuding houses of Montague and Capulet.
In a famous balcony scene the heroine asks, "wherefore art thou?"
Its prologue calls its title characters "a pair of star-cross’d lovers."
Name this Shakespeare tragedy about two young lovers.
In this epic the hero blinds the Cyclops Polyphemus and escapes beneath the bellies of sheep.
Its hero lashes himself to a mast to hear the Sirens and resists the enchantress Circe.
His wife Penelope holds off suitors by weaving and unweaving a shroud.
It follows the ten-year homeward voyage of Odysseus to Ithaca.
Name this ancient Greek epic attributed to Homer.
In this novel the destitute ex-student Raskolnikov tests a theory that extraordinary men may transgress moral law.
He murders a pawnbroker and her sister with an axe, then is tormented by guilt.
The detective Porfiry Petrovich slowly closes in, and the devout Sonya urges confession.
It is set in the slums of St. Petersburg.
Name this Fyodor Dostoevsky novel.
This novel is narrated by a girl nicknamed Scout in the fictional town of Maycomb, Alabama.
A reclusive neighbor, Boo Radley, leaves gifts in a tree hollow.
Its lawyer protagonist Atticus Finch defends Tom Robinson, a Black man falsely accused of rape.
Its title alludes to the sin of killing a harmless songbird.
Name this Harper Lee novel.
This novel’s deluded hero, inspired by chivalric romances, renames a farm nag Rocinante and idealizes a peasant as Dulcinea.
He charges windmills he mistakes for giants.
His pragmatic squire is Sancho Panza.
It is set largely in La Mancha, Spain.
Name this Miguel de Cervantes novel, often called the first modern novel.
This novel’s narrator wanders New York after being expelled from Pencey Prep, wondering where the ducks in Central Park go in winter.
He wears a red hunting hat and rails against "phonies."
Its narrator is the alienated teenager Holden Caulfield.
Its title comes from his fantasy of catching children in a field of rye.
Name this J. D. Salinger novel.
This novel opens as Colonel Aureliano Buendía faces a firing squad and recalls the day his father took him to discover ice.
It chronicles seven generations of the Buendía family in the town of Macondo.
A defining work of magical realism, it ends as the town is swept away by wind.
Its author is Colombian.
Name this Gabriel García Márquez novel.
reject: Love in the Time of Cholera

clues: This author created the convict Magwitch and the orphan Pip in "Great Expectations."
His novel of the French Revolution opens, "It was the best of times, it was the worst of times."
In one novella the miser Ebenezer Scrooge is visited by three spirits.
He also wrote "Oliver Twist" and "David Copperfield."
Name this Victorian English novelist of "A Christmas Carol."
This author, born Samuel Clemens, took his pen name from a riverboat call meaning two fathoms deep.
He set boyhood adventures in the town of St. Petersburg, modeled on his native Hannibal, Missouri.
One of his heroes rafts down the Mississippi with a runaway named Jim.
He wrote "The Adventures of Tom Sawyer."
Name this American author of "Adventures of Huckleberry Finn."
This naturalist developed his ideas after a five-year voyage aboard HMS Beagle.
He noted that finches on the Galápagos Islands had beaks suited to different foods.
His 1859 book is "On the Origin of Species."
He proposed evolution by natural selection.
Name this English naturalist often paired with the phrase "survival of the fittest."
This scientist coined the term "radioactivity" while studying uranium ores with her husband.
She discovered the elements polonium—named for her homeland—and radium.
She remains the only person to win Nobel Prizes in two different sciences.
She won the Nobel Prizes in Physics (1903) and Chemistry (1911).
Name this Polish-born physicist and chemist.
This chemist left gaps in his great table and predicted the properties of undiscovered elements like "eka-aluminium," later found as gallium.
He arranged the known elements by increasing atomic weight and recurring properties.
His name is given to element 101.
He is credited with formulating the periodic table.
Name this 19th-century Russian chemist.
This Augustinian friar cross-bred some 28,000 plants in a monastery garden in Brno.
He described "dominant" and "recessive" factors and ratios like 3 to 1.
His experiments used the pea plant.
His laws of inheritance founded the science of genetics.
Name this "father of genetics."
This scientist’s swan-neck flask experiments refuted spontaneous generation.
He developed vaccines for anthrax and rabies.
He championed the germ theory of disease.
A gentle heating process for milk is named for him.
Name this French microbiologist behind "pasteurization."
Georges Lemaître first proposed this idea as a "primeval atom."
The 1964 discovery of the cosmic microwave background radiation supported it.
Edwin Hubble’s observation that distant galaxies are receding pointed toward it.
It dates the origin of the universe to about 13.8 billion years ago.
Name this leading theory for the expanding universe’s beginning.
Alfred Wegener proposed an early version of this theory, citing matching fossils and coastlines across oceans.
It holds that a supercontinent called Pangaea once existed and later broke apart.
New crust forms at mid-ocean ridges and is consumed at subduction zones.
It explains why earthquakes and volcanoes cluster at the edges of rigid plates.
Name this theory of the slow motion of Earth’s lithospheric plates.
Joseph Priestley and Carl Scheele independently isolated this gas, which Antoine Lavoisier later named.
With atomic number 8, it makes up about 21 percent of Earth’s atmosphere.
It is required for combustion and for animal respiration.
Its diatomic molecule is written O₂.
Name this element with chemical symbol O.
This conflict was sparked by the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand in Sarajevo in 1914.
Much of it was fought from entrenched lines along the Western Front.
It introduced widespread use of poison gas and tanks.
It ended in 1918 and was formalized by the Treaty of Versailles.
Name this global conflict once called "the war to end all wars."
A committee of five, with Thomas Jefferson as principal author, drafted this document.
It lists grievances against King George III.
It asserts that all men are "endowed by their Creator with certain unalienable Rights."
It was adopted by the Continental Congress on July 4, 1776.
Name this founding American document proclaiming separation from Britain.
Born Octavian, this man was the grand-nephew and heir of Julius Caesar.
He defeated Mark Antony and Cleopatra at the naval Battle of Actium.
His reign began the long peace known as the Pax Romana.
He is considered the first Roman emperor.
Name this ruler whose honorific became a title and named a month.
This man reputedly nailed 95 Theses to a church door in Wittenberg in 1517.
Summoned to the Diet of Worms, he refused to recant, saying "Here I stand."
He translated the Bible into German.
He launched the Protestant Reformation.
Name this German monk, not to be confused with the American civil-rights leader.
This explorer’s voyage was financed by Ferdinand and Isabella of Spain.
His three ships were the Niña, the Pinta, and the Santa María.
Believing he had reached the Indies, he called the inhabitants "Indians."
He reached the Americas in 1492.
Name this Genoese explorer with a U.S. holiday in October.
This upheaval began as the Third Estate broke from the Estates-General.
A Parisian crowd stormed the Bastille prison on July 14, 1789.
Its radical phase, the Reign of Terror, sent thousands to the guillotine under Robespierre.
Its watchwords were "Liberty, Equality, Fraternity."
Name this late-18th-century revolution that toppled the monarchy of Louis XVI.
This leader first developed his methods fighting discrimination as a lawyer in South Africa.
In 1930 he led a 240-mile march to the sea to make salt in defiance of British law.
He called his philosophy of nonviolent resistance "satyagraha."
He guided India toward independence in 1947.
Name this figure honored with the title "Mahatma."
As a youth this ruler was tutored by Aristotle and tamed the horse Bucephalus.
He is said to have undone the Gordian Knot with his sword.
He shattered the Persian army of Darius III and founded cities named for himself.
He was king of Macedon and reportedly never lost a battle.
Name this conqueror styled "the Great."
This country’s highest peak is the sacred volcano Mount Fuji.
It is an archipelago whose main islands include Honshu and Hokkaido.
Its capital is Tokyo.
It is nicknamed the "Land of the Rising Sun."
Name this East Asian island nation.
Roald Amundsen’s team reached this region’s central point in 1911, just ahead of Robert Falcon Scott.
It has no permanent human population and is governed by an international treaty.
It contains the geographic South Pole.
It is the coldest, driest, and windiest continent.
Name this ice-covered southernmost continent.
This landform exposes nearly two billion years of rock strata in its walls.
It was carved over millions of years by the Colorado River.
It lies mostly within a national park in the state of Arizona.
It is roughly 277 miles long and up to a mile deep.
Name this enormous American gorge.
Pliny the Younger described this volcano’s eruption in letters, a type now called "Plinian."
Its 79 AD eruption buried the cities of Pompeii and Herculaneum in ash.
It overlooks the Bay of Naples.
It is the only active volcano on mainland Europe.
Name this Italian volcano.
This city was once a powerful maritime republic ruled by a doge.
It is built across more than 100 small islands in a lagoon.
Its Grand Canal is traversed by gondolas.
Its central square is dominated by St. Mark’s Basilica.
Name this canal-filled city in northeastern Italy.
Its shores mark the lowest exposed land on Earth, over 400 meters below sea level.
It borders Jordan to the east and Israel and the West Bank to the west.
Its extreme salinity lets bathers float effortlessly.
Its name reflects the fact that fish cannot survive in it.
Name this hypersaline lake in the Jordan Rift Valley.
The acronym HOMES is a mnemonic for these five bodies of water.
The largest, Superior, is the world’s largest freshwater lake by area.
They straddle the border of the United States and Canada.
They are Superior, Michigan, Huron, Erie, and Ontario.
Name this group of five large North American lakes.
This country’s purpose-built capital, Brasília, opened in 1960.
A statue of Christ the Redeemer overlooks its city of Rio de Janeiro.
It is the only major Portuguese-speaking country in the Americas.
Most of the Amazon rainforest lies within it.
Name this largest country in South America.
This artist collaborated with Luis Buñuel on the film "Un Chien Andalou."
Famous for a flamboyant waxed mustache, he called his method "paranoiac-critical."
His best-known canvas depicts limp, melting pocket watches.
That work is "The Persistence of Memory."
Name this Spanish Surrealist painter.
A near-fatal bus accident at 18 left this artist in lifelong pain that shaped her work.
She married the muralist Diego Rivera.
Her many self-portraits feature a distinctive joined eyebrow and Mexican folk dress.
They include "The Two Fridas."
Name this Mexican painter.
This artist ran a New York studio nicknamed "The Factory."
He is credited with the idea of "15 minutes of fame."
He silkscreened repeated images of Marilyn Monroe and Campbell’s Soup Cans.
He was a leading figure of Pop Art.
Name this American artist with platinum-wig fame.
This artist painted dozens of searching self-portraits across his life.
A master of light and shadow, he also produced celebrated etchings.
His group portrait of a militia company is popularly called "The Night Watch."
He was the leading painter of the Dutch Golden Age.
Name this 17th-century Dutch master, usually known by his first name.
This artist left only about 35 known paintings, many quiet domestic interiors lit from a left-hand window.
His works include "The Milkmaid" and "The Art of Painting."
He worked in the Dutch city of Delft.
His best-known portrait wears a blue-and-gold turban and a single luminous earring.
Name this painter of "Girl with a Pearl Earring."
This artist laid huge canvases on the floor and worked from all sides.
His technique of pouring and flinging paint earned the nickname "Jack the Dripper."
He was a central figure of American Abstract Expressionism.
His drip paintings carry titles like "No. 5, 1948."
Name this American "action painter."
This sculptor’s unfinished "The Gates of Hell" spawned many independent figures.
His "The Kiss" depicts two entwined lovers in marble.
A seated, brooding nude is his most reproduced work.
That bronze is called "The Thinker."
Name this French sculptor.
Critics mocked this artist and his circle as "fauves," or wild beasts, for their wild color.
Late in life, confined to bed, he made art from cut painted paper.
Those cut-outs include the book "Jazz" and "The Snail."
His paintings include two versions of "The Dance."
Name this French pioneer of Fauvism.
This composer wrote almost exclusively for solo piano.
His works include nocturnes, études, and dances evoking his homeland, such as polonaises and mazurkas.
A brisk waltz of his is nicknamed the "Minute Waltz."
Born in Poland, he spent his career largely in Paris.
Name this Romantic composer and pianist.
This singer recorded his early hits at Sun Studio in Memphis.
His gyrating performances earned him the nickname "Elvis the Pelvis."
His hits include "Heartbreak Hotel" and "Jailhouse Rock."
He lived at a Memphis mansion called Graceland.
Name this singer crowned the "King of Rock and Roll."
This band’s 1985 Live Aid set is often called one of rock’s greatest performances.
Its guitarist Brian May built his own guitar, the "Red Special."
A six-minute mock-operatic single of theirs has no chorus.
Its flamboyant frontman was Freddie Mercury.
Name this British rock band behind "Bohemian Rhapsody."
This performer began his career as the youngest frontman of a Motown family group.
He popularized a backward-gliding dance step called the moonwalk.
His 1982 album remains the best-selling of all time.
That album, "Thriller," includes "Billie Jean" and "Beat It."
Name this entertainer dubbed the "King of Pop."
An ordained priest with red hair, this composer was nicknamed "the Red Priest."
He taught music at a Venetian orphanage for girls, the Pio Ospedale della Pietà.
He wrote hundreds of concertos, many for violin.
His best-known set of four concertos depicts the seasons.
Name this Italian Baroque composer of "The Four Seasons."
Born Robert Zimmerman, this musician took his stage surname reputedly from a poet.
He shocked folk purists by "going electric" at the 1965 Newport Folk Festival.
His protest anthems include "Blowin’ in the Wind" and "The Times They Are a-Changin’."
In 2016 he won the Nobel Prize in Literature.
Name this American singer-songwriter of "Like a Rolling Stone."
This New Orleans musician led the Hot Five and Hot Seven recording groups.
His gravelly voice and trumpet virtuosity helped popularize scat singing.
He was nicknamed "Satchmo" and "Pops."
His late hits include "What a Wonderful World" and "Hello, Dolly!"
Name this foundational jazz trumpeter and singer.
This German-born composer spent most of his career in London writing Italian operas.
His orchestral suites include the "Water Music" and "Music for the Royal Fireworks."
His most famous oratorio premiered in Dublin in 1742.
That work contains the "Hallelujah" chorus.
Name this Baroque composer of "Messiah."
This god overthrew his father Cronus and drew the sky as his domain by lot.
His many disguises in pursuit of mortals included a swan and a bull.
He wields the thunderbolt as his weapon.
He is king of the gods atop Mount Olympus.
Name this chief Greek god, called Jupiter by the Romans.
This god gave one of his eyes for a drink from the well of wisdom.
Two ravens, Huginn and Muninn, fly the world and report to him.
Slain warriors feast in his hall, Valhalla.
He is the chief god of Norse mythology and father of Thor.
Name this Allfather of the Norse pantheon.
Driven mad by Hera, this hero killed his family and was set penance to atone.
For his first task he strangled an invulnerable lion and wore its hide.
He performed Twelve Labors, including capturing Cerberus.
He was the half-divine son of Zeus, famed for superhuman strength.
Name this hero, known in Greek as Heracles.
One myth says Athena cursed this once-beautiful woman after a desecration of her temple.
She was the only mortal of her three monstrous sisters.
A hero used a mirrored shield to behead her without looking directly at her.
Anyone who met her gaze turned to stone, and snakes grew from her head.
Name this Gorgon slain by Perseus.
This god rides a chariot pulled by two goats and wears a belt that doubles his strength.
His chief foe is the world-serpent Jörmungandr, whom he is fated to slay and die beside.
He wields the hammer Mjölnir.
He is the Norse god of thunder.
Name this hammer-wielding god, son of Odin.
This deity is shown weighing a dead person’s heart against the feather of Ma’at.
He oversaw embalming and the rites of mummification.
He is depicted with the black head of a jackal.
He guided souls in the Egyptian underworld.
Name this jackal-headed Egyptian god of the dead.
Each entry in this saga opens with a yellow text crawl receding into space.
Its mystical energy field, "the Force," binds the galaxy together.
A farm boy named Luke Skywalker confronts the masked Darth Vader.
It was created by George Lucas, beginning with a 1977 film.
Name this space-opera franchise set "a long time ago in a galaxy far, far away."
This film, adapted from a Mario Puzo novel, was directed by Francis Ford Coppola.
Marlon Brando won an Oscar as its aging patriarch, Vito Corleone.
A character promises to make someone "an offer he can’t refuse."
It follows the Corleone Mafia family, with Al Pacino as son Michael.
Name this 1972 crime epic.
This director made cameo appearances in nearly all of his films.
His thrillers include "Rear Window," "Vertigo," and "The Birds."
One of his films features a notorious shower-stabbing scene scored with shrieking violins.
That film is "Psycho."
Name this English "Master of Suspense."
This film’s newspaper magnate was loosely modeled on William Randolph Hearst.
A reporter investigates the meaning of the title character’s dying word.
That word, "Rosebud," is revealed to name a childhood sled.
It was co-written, directed by, and stars Orson Welles.
Name this 1941 film often ranked the greatest of all time.
This film shifts from sepia to vivid Technicolor when its heroine arrives in a strange land.
A girl named Dorothy follows a yellow brick road with a dog named Toto.
She clicks together a pair of ruby slippers, repeating "There’s no place like home."
Its songs include "Over the Rainbow."
Name this 1939 MGM musical based on an L. Frank Baum book.
This film is widely credited with inventing the summer blockbuster.
A two-note John Williams motif signals the menace.
A police chief, an oceanographer, and a grizzled fisherman hunt the threat off Amity Island.
A character quips, "You’re gonna need a bigger boat."
Name this 1975 Steven Spielberg film about a great white shark.
Pierre de Coubertin revived these events in Athens in 1896.
Their Latin motto is "Citius, Altius, Fortius" — faster, higher, stronger.
Their symbol is five interlocking rings.
In antiquity they were held at Olympia in honor of Zeus.
Name this international sporting competition held every four years.
Born Cassius Clay, this boxer changed his name after joining the Nation of Islam.
He beat George Foreman in Zaire in the "Rumble in the Jungle."
He promised to "float like a butterfly, sting like a bee."
He was a three-time world heavyweight champion.
Name this boxer who called himself "The Greatest."
Born Edson Arantes do Nascimento, this athlete starred for the club Santos.
He scored over a thousand career goals and later played for the New York Cosmos.
He won three World Cups, the first as a teenager in 1958.
He played the sport called football outside the United States.
Name this Brazilian soccer legend.
This athlete briefly retired to play minor-league baseball before returning to his sport.
Wearing number 23, he won six championships with the Chicago Bulls.
A signature line of Nike sneakers bears his name.
He is widely called the greatest basketball player of all time.
Name this athlete nicknamed "Air."
This player began his career as a star left-handed pitcher for the Boston Red Sox.
His sale to a rival team supposedly began an 86-year championship drought, "the Curse" of his nickname.
Nicknamed "the Bambino" and "the Sultan of Swat," he is said to have "called his shot" in the 1932 World Series.
He set a long-standing home run record with the New York Yankees.
Name this baseball legend.
